input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, a six-axis serial robot used in machining operations. Key components include motors, gearboxes, and the first vertical axis at the base, which is specifically modeled for digital shadow creation. The system's operational domain involves precision motion control in industrial machining environments where dynamic joint behavior and friction characteristics are critical for performance.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real-time monitoring through live-data linking, visualization via a dashboard interface, and continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction to enable predictive maintenance and performance optimization. These services target maintenance engineers and system operators, offering actionable insights for reducing downtime and improving operational efficiency through data-driven decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for system complexity management. Modeling is conducted using SysML across requirements, functional, logical, and technical abstraction layers. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define component interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling is handled via MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, integrated with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed behavior specification and heterogeneous stakeholder integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system is structured using the Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1), a UML class diagram defining components including Asset (physical entity), Model (mathematical representation), Processing (data transformation logic), and DataTrace (sensor data streams). The Concrete Digital Shadow Model (Fig. 5) implements these elements with real-time data streaming via MQTT and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized data exchange within the manufacturing context.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include structural representations via SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) and ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc), behavioral models using MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, and analytical simulations via MathWorks Simulink, C++, or Java. Data is sourced from physical system states through automated data flows, managed by integrating heterogeneous modeling tools and methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) for multi-level abstraction across requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor time series data is streamed from physical assets to the digital system using MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for sensor data transmission. Network topology, bandwidth requirements, latency specifications, reliability and redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system complies with industry standards including SysML [17] for system modeling, ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25,26], and SysML IBDs [17,27] as architecture description languages (ADLs) for component and connector perspectives. Additionally, methodologies such as RFLP [18,19], OOSEM [20], SPES [21], and SMArDT [22] are applied to manage complexity across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer).

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot as the physical system, its six-axis nature, and its application in machining. It correctly identifies key components like motors and gearboxes, specifically mentioning the first vertical axis for the digital shadow. It also notes the operational domain of precision motion control and the criticality of dynamic joint behavior and friction. This is well-supported by the text: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining', 'The first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow', and 'modeling the friction in the robot joints'.

The extraction correctly lists real-time monitoring (live-data linking), visualization (dashboard), and continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction. It infers predictive maintenance and performance optimization as benefits. While the paper mentions 'improve the robot’s motion accuracy' and 'understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss', the terms 'predictive maintenance' and 'downtime' are not explicitly found in the text, making the description slightly speculative in parts. However, the core services are strongly supported: 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard' and 'enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction'.

The extraction correctly identifies the use of Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), SysML, and MathWorks Simulink, which are confirmed in the text as used for modeling and simulation. However, it lists specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) as being employed by the implementation, whereas the text presents them merely as examples of existing methodologies in the background section ('e.g., RFLP...'). While technically detailed, this conflation of background examples with specific implementation tools prevents a perfect score. Evidence: 'Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink', 'described by the SysML SystemModel'.

Finally, the RFLP methodology is explicitly mentioned: 'modeling is typically split into different levels of abstraction... e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19]'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents provide clear descriptions of physical acting components. Section III details the 'MABI MAX 100' robot, specifically describing the first axis which 'is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor'. It further specifies the actuation chain: 'a gear transmission is first used... followed by a cycloidal gear'. Additionally, it describes the control capabilities: 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl... The axes can be controlled separately. Position, speed, or acceleration can serve as inputs.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document', however, Section III explicitly describes the sensing infrastructure. It states: 'There are two position encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.' It also mentions the capabilities of these sensors: 'This sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional uncertainty'. Section IV further adds that 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT. These are the input current and the output angle.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on the methodology for constructing digital shadows and integrating white-box and black-box models. While Section IV mentions comparing 'simulated data versus measured data' to understand friction loss, and Section I mentions improving 'robot motion accuracy', the text does not contain specific details regarding formal 'fidelity requirements', 'validation and verification methods', 'uncertainty sources and quantification', or 'error detection and correction mechanisms'. Therefore, the statement that this information is not in the document is correct.
